question: What casualty type occurred and on what date and local time, according to the Casualty Summary?
reference_answer: Capsizing/Listing, on May 13, 2024, about 1657 central daylight time.
supporting_source_spans: Opening paragraph: 'about 1657 local time, the towing vessel Baylor J. Tregre was towing the ocean barge MARMAC 27 ... capsized and sank.'

question: What was loaded on barge MARMAC 27 for this voyage, and what was the intended destination?
reference_answer: MARMAC 27 was loaded with a production platform and helideck, to be towed from Houma, Louisiana, to Brazos Area Block 538A, an oil and gas lease block in the Gulf of America.
supporting_source_spans: Section 1.2 Event Sequence: 'loaded with a production platform and helideck, from Houma, Louisiana, to Brazos Area Block 538A'

question: What does the NTSB determine as the probable cause of the capsizing and sinking?
reference_answer: The mate's inability to maneuver the tow into the wind due to the overwhelming towline force generated by the towed barge during the sudden onset of severe weather, resulting in unrecoverable heeling.

question: What was the estimated damage to the towing vessel, and what environmental damage was reported?
reference_answer: Damage to the towing vessel was estimated at $2 million. Environmental damage: None.
supporting_source_spans: Opening paragraph: 'There was no pollution reported. Damage to the towing vessel was estimated at $2 million.'

question: What pollution was reported following the casualty?
reference_answer: There was no pollution reported.

question: What is the NTSB case number for this investigation and what is the marine investigation report number?
reference_answer: NTSB case number DCA24FM038; Marine Investigation Report MIR-25-21.
supporting_source_spans: CAROL reference at end of document

question: Who built the Baylor J. Tregre, in what year, in what city/state, and under what original name?
reference_answer: Built by R&S Fabricators, Inc. in Lockport, Louisiana, in 1997, as the Trent Joseph (for Coastal Towing LLC). Renamed Baylor J. Tregre after Trinity Tugs LLC acquired the vessel in 2023.

question: What entities are named as owner/operator of the Baylor J. Tregre, owner of MARMAC 27, and lessee of MARMAC 27?
reference_answer: Owner/operator of the Baylor J. Tregre: Trinity Tugs LLC (Commercial). Owner of MARMAC 27: McDonough Marine Service. Lessee of MARMAC 27: Manson Construction Co.

question: When did the Baylor J. Tregre depart Houma, Louisiana, and how many days elapsed before the casualty?
reference_answer: The Baylor J. Tregre departed Houma about 0340 on May 11, 2024, en route to Block 538A. The casualty occurred about 1657 on May 13, 2024, so the casualty occurred on the third calendar day of the voyage (approximately 2 days, 13 hours, and 17 minutes after departure).
supporting_source_spans: Section 1.2 Event Sequence: 'The same day, about 0340, the Baylor J. Tregre departed Houma'

question: According to AIS data, how did the vessel's speed change between 1647:04 and 1654:20, and what value is reported at each timestamp?
reference_answer: According to AIS data, the vessel's speed dropped from 4.0 knots at 1647:04 to 1.8 knots at 1650:09, and further to 0.6 knots at about 1654:20.
supporting_source_spans: Section 1.2 Event Sequence: 'vessel's speed dropped from 4.0 knots at 1647:04 to 1.8 knots at 1650:09'
'About 1654:20, the vessel's speed had dropped to 0.6 knots, according to AIS.'

question: Reconstruct the chronological sequence of National Weather Service products issued on May 13, 2024, including times and product types referenced in the report.
reference_answer: 1257: NWS issued a severe thunderstorm watch (effective until 2000) warning of wind gusts 60-80 knots and large hail. 1409: NWS issued a Coastal Waters Forecast for High Island to Matagorda Ship Channel out 60 miles. 1501: NWS issued a Special Marine Warning for severe thunderstorms, wind gusts to 40 knots, large hail, frequent lightning, advising mariners to 'move to safe harbor'. 1544: Special Marine Warning was updated, reporting 'strong thunderstorms' with wind gusts 34 knots or greater and small hail.

question: According to the report, when did salvage operations begin, when did they end, and on what date was the vessel transported to the salvage dock in Galveston?
reference_answer: Salvage operations were conducted from May 18 to June 15, 2024. The vessel was transported to a salvage dock in Galveston, Texas, on June 12, 2024.
supporting_source_spans: Section 1.2 Event Sequence near end: 'Salvage operations were conducted from May 18 to June 15. The salvor found the Baylor J. Tregre in an inverted position on the ocean floor. The vessel was successfully raised from the seafloor and transported to a salvage dock in Galveston, Texas, on June 12.'

question: What were the principal dimensions of the Baylor J. Tregre (length overall, breadth, draft at casualty, gross tonnage, engine power and manufacturer)?
reference_answer: Length overall: 67.2 ft (20.4 m). Breadth (max.): 24.0 ft (7.3 m). Draft (casualty): 9.0 ft (2.7 m). Tonnage: 97 GRT. Engine power: 2 x 750 hp (559 kW); Caterpillar 3412 diesel engines.

question: How much tow wire was on the winch, and how long was the towline actually deployed between the tug and the barge?
reference_answer: The towing winch carried 1,800 feet of 1.25-inch tow wire. The main tow wire (towline) actually deployed between the tug and the barge was about 800 feet long.
supporting_source_spans: Section 1.1 Background: '1,800 feet of 1.25-inch tow wire'
Section 1.2 Event Sequence: 'the main tow wire (towline), which was about 800 feet long'

question: What conditions were reported at KGLS, the nearest NWS station, regarding thunderstorm start time, maximum wind gust, end time, and total rainfall?
reference_answer: Thunderstorms began at KGLS at 1554. Winds gusted up to 48 knots at 1724. Thunderstorms ended at 1734. About 0.85 inches of rainfall was recorded.

question: What were the casualty site coordinates and the water depth at the casualty site?
reference_answer: Coordinates: 28°56.34' N, 094°54.7' W. Water depth at the casualty site: 60 ft. The site was about 23 nautical miles south of Galveston, Texas.

question: Who issued the Special Marine Warning at 1501, what wind gusts did it forecast, and what action did it advise mariners to take?
reference_answer: The National Weather Service (NWS) issued the Special Marine Warning at 1501. It forecast severe thunderstorms capable of wind gusts to 40 knots, large hail, and frequent lightning, and advised mariners to 'move to safe harbor until hazardous weather passes.'
supporting_source_spans: Section 1.3.3.2 Weather Forecasts: 'At 1501, the NWS issued a Special Marine Warning ...'

question: Who provided the satellite cloud-top data referenced in the report for 1656, and from what satellite source?
reference_answer: The Space Science Engineering Center at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, in Madison, Wisconsin, provided satellite data; the data referenced was from the GOES-16 satellite.
supporting_source_spans: Figure 8 caption: 'Background source: GOES-16 satellite data from Space Science Engineering Center'

question: Who conducted the suitability survey on the Baylor J. Tregre, on what date, in what city, and at whose request?
reference_answer: A surveyor (unnamed in the report) completed the suitability survey on April 22, 2024, in Houma, at the request of the company that would receive the production platform and helideck on the MARMAC 27.

question: The captain estimated the winds were 85–100 mph (about 74–87 knots) at the time of the casualty. By contrast, what wind gusts did the nearest NWS observation locations report, and how far from the casualty site were those locations?
reference_answer: The captain estimated winds were 85-100 mph (74-87 knots), but the closest NWS location reported winds gusting up to 48-62 knots. KGLS (22 miles north) reported up to 48 knots; GNJT2 (Galveston Bay north jetty buoy, 27 miles from the casualty site) reported a 62-knot gust; KGVW (Galveston Offshore 209A Buoy, about 25 miles northeast) reported wind from 310 degrees at 28 knots gusting to 40 knots. The Analysis section explicitly notes this discrepancy.
supporting_source_spans: Section 2 Analysis: 'The captain estimated winds were about 85-100 mph (about 74-87 knots), and the closest NWS location reported winds gusting up to 48-62 knots (22-27 miles from the casualty site).'

question: The report identifies multiple openings through which the vessel could have flooded after losing stability. List them and indicate which condition is described as deteriorated before the storm versus damaged by the storm.
reference_answer: Three sets of openings are identified in the Analysis section. (1) Fiddley blowers (on the second deck) - found missing when the vessel was salvaged (storm/casualty-related). (2) Windows - blown open during the storm (storm-related). (3) Exterior doors - with hinges and sealing gaskets found deteriorated after the casualty (described as a 'lesser degree' contributor; the deterioration of hinges/gaskets was a pre-existing condition). The report also notes the starboard-side engine room exterior door was found separated from its hinges on the deck and the forward starboard main deck door was found open before salvage.
supporting_source_spans: Section 2 Analysis paragraph on flooding openings

question: Even if the mate had received the Special Marine Warnings, the report states the crew could not have avoided the storm. What three factors does the report cite?
reference_answer: The report cites three factors: (1) the vessel's speed (about 4-5 knots); (2) the tow's position (about 17 miles from Galveston and surrounded by storms); and (3) the speed of the storm's advance.
supporting_source_spans: Section 2 Analysis: 'even if the Baylor J. Tregre crew had received the Special Marine Warnings, they would not have been able to avoid the storm, given the vessel's speed (about 4-5 knots), the tow's position (about 17 miles from Galveston and surrounded by storms), and the speed of the storm's advance.'

question: Did the suitability survey specify weather criteria limits for the tow of MARMAC 27?
reference_answer: No. The survey recommended 'Careful consideration is to be given to weather criteria for the tow of MARMAC 27,' but the report explicitly states the survey did not specify weather criteria limits for the tow.
supporting_source_spans: Section 1.3.2 Vessel Inspections and Survey: 'The survey did not specify weather criteria limits for the tow.'

question: What does the report say about the IMO number and classification society of the Baylor J. Tregre?
reference_answer: Both fields are listed as N/A (not applicable). The Vessel Particulars table records IMO number: N/A and Classification society: N/A.

question: Combining the Event Sequence and Analysis sections, what three conditions does the report give for why the crew was unable to release the tow before the vessel capsized?
reference_answer: The Analysis section states three reasons. (1) The Baylor J. Tregre wheelhouse had no emergency release function, nor was one required under 46 CFR Subchapter M. (2) The doghouse, which housed the winch controls, was partially submerged and inaccessible. (3) Even if the mate could have reached the doghouse, the tow winch drive engine was not running, and the upper engine room (where the engine would need to be started) was itself inaccessible because the vessel was heeling so far to port. The Event Sequence corroborates these conditions: the mate found the doghouse halfway underwater with windows blown out, returned to the wheelhouse, and reported there was nothing he could do.
supporting_source_spans: Section 1.2 Event Sequence (mate's attempt to reach the doghouse)
Section 2 Analysis (paragraph on releasing the tow, with footnote 8 citing 46 CFR Subchapter M)
